specific_location: Barr Lake State Park banding station
description: The bird had a reddish olive crown, gray supercilium, and rufous auriculars. The malar stripes were dark rufous and widest at the posterior end. Its upperparts were grayish olive with heavy rufous streaking, and the rump and uppertail coverts were rufous brown. The throat and underparts were white with distinct, triangular-shaped, reddish brown breast spots more clustered at the center of the breast and becoming darker in color but less numerous toward the belly. The whitish wing bars were indistinct and narrow. The bill was large with an exposed culmen of 12.0mm and width at nares of 8.0mm. The mandible was yellow, and the maxilla and tip of the bill were both dark. Based on the overall reddish plumage, streaked back, and wing chord (85 mm) that measured larger than the tail (73 mm), this Fox Sparrow belongs to the Northern Red Group (P. i. iliaca). This FOSP was aged as Hatching Year because its skull was incompletely pneumaticized.Behaviors: Normal behaviors of the Fox Sparrow were not observed during its capture and banding.Call: not applicablePlumage: reddish brown upperpts spotted breast
photographer: Photographer: Glenda Brown, Glenda Brown, Glenda Brown, Glenda Brown
